questions.question: What is a logical gate?

options.text: A gate that operates on classical bits
A gate that operates on encoded (logical) qubits while maintaining the code space
Any quantum gate applied to physical qubits
A gate that decodes qubits before operating

questions.explanation: A logical gate operates on encoded qubits, transforming the logical state correctly while keeping the state in the code space. It acts on the logical qubit represented by multiple physical qubits, not on individual physical qubits.

questions.question: What is the main fault tolerance requirement for logical gates?

options.text: Gates must be very fast
A single fault should cause at most one error per code block
Gates must use only one physical qubit
All gates must be Clifford gates

questions.explanation: The key fault tolerance requirement is that a single fault (error) during a gate should cause at most one error in each output code block. This ensures errors stay within the code's correction capacity.

questions.question: Which gates form the Clifford group?

options.text: Only Pauli gates (X, Y, Z)
Hadamard, Phase (S), CNOT, and all Paulis
Only the T gate
All possible quantum gates

questions.explanation: The Clifford group includes all Pauli gates (X, Y, Z, I), plus Hadamard (H), Phase (S), and CNOT. These are gates that map Pauli operators to Pauli operators under conjugation.

questions.question: Why is the T gate important?

options.text: It's the simplest quantum gate
Combined with Clifford gates, it provides a universal gate set
It's always transversal
It can correct errors directly

questions.explanation: The T gate is crucial because Clifford gates alone are classically simulable and not universal. Adding the T gate (a non-Clifford gate) to the Clifford group creates a universal gate set capable of approximating any quantum operation.

questions.question: What does the Eastin-Knill theorem state?

options.text: All gates can be implemented transversally
No stabilizer code has a transversal universal gate set
Clifford gates are always fault-tolerant
Magic states are unnecessary

questions.explanation: The Eastin-Knill theorem proves that no stabilizer code can have a transversal universal gate set. This is why techniques like magic state injection or code switching are needed to achieve universality.

questions.question: What is a transversal gate?

options.text: A gate that acts between different code blocks
A gate implemented by applying the same physical gate to each qubit in parallel
A gate that requires ancilla qubits
A gate that only works on surface codes

questions.explanation: A transversal gate applies the same physical gate to each qubit of the code block in parallel (U_L = U^⊗n). This structure automatically ensures fault tolerance since errors can't spread between qubits.

questions.question: Clifford gates alone are sufficient for universal quantum computation.
questions.explanation: False. Clifford gates alone are classically simulable (Gottesman-Knill theorem) and cannot perform universal quantum computation. A non-Clifford gate like T is required in addition to achieve universality.
